tasks.title: Count Vowels and Consonants
tasks.description:
Strings are often analyzed character by character to extract useful information, such as how many letters of each type appear in a message. This kind of scan is common in text processing, spell-checking, and simple language tools.

Read one line of text with at most 100 characters, including spaces. Count how many alphabetic characters are vowels and how many are consonants, treating both uppercase and lowercase letters as valid. Print the two counts on one line in the format `vowels consonants`. Characters that are not letters must be ignored.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `Hello, World!` → vowels: 0 → 3, consonants: 0 → 7. Output: `3 7`

tasks.title: Reverse Each Word
tasks.description:
Working with text often requires rearranging characters while keeping the structure of the sentence. Reversing words individually is useful in puzzle games, text effects, and basic formatting tasks.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Reverse the letters in each word while keeping the words in the same order and preserving every space exactly as it appears in the input. Print the transformed line followed by a newline. Words are maximal consecutive sequences of non-space characters, and the input may contain leading, trailing, or multiple spaces.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `one  two` → `one` becomes `eno` and `two` becomes `owt`. Output: `eno  owt`

tasks.title: Longest Run of a Character
tasks.description:
When analyzing text, it is useful to know how strongly repeated characters cluster together. This idea appears in data compression, signal inspection, and simple pattern detection.

Read one line of text with at most 100 characters. Find the length of the longest consecutive block of the same character. Print a single integer. If the line is empty or contains no characters before the newline, print `0`.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `aaabbccccd` → longest block length: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4. Output: `4`

tasks.title: Remove Duplicate Letters
tasks.description:
Text cleaning tools often need to keep only the first occurrence of each character while preserving order. This is a simple form of filtering used in deduplication and compact labeling.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Print a new line containing only the first occurrence of each character from the input, in the same order they first appear. The comparison is case-sensitive, so `A` and `a` are different. The newline at the end of the input should not be included in the output.

tasks.title: Count Words by Length
tasks.description:
The number of words of different sizes can tell you a lot about a sentence’s style. Text analysis tools often group words by length before summarizing or comparing writing.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. A word is a maximal sequence of letters only, where letters may be uppercase or lowercase. Count how many words have length 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 or more. Print the five counts on one line separated by spaces. Ignore any non-letter characters as word separators.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `Hi, I am Sam!` → word lengths: 2 → 1 → 2 → 3, so the counts change to `1 2 1 0 0`. Output: `1 2 1 0 0`

tasks.title: Replace Digits with Hash Marks
tasks.description:
Character-by-character transformation is a basic part of sanitizing text before display or storage. Programs that mask sensitive information often replace certain categories of characters while leaving the rest unchanged.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Produce a copy of the line where every digit character (`0` to `9`) is replaced by `#`, and all other characters remain the same. Print the transformed line followed by a newline. The input line may be empty.

tasks.title: Compare Two Strings Ignoring Case
tasks.description:
Text comparisons are easier when differences in letter case should not matter. This is common in login checks, dictionary lookups, and many search features.

Read two separate lines, each with at most 100 characters, excluding the newline. Compare them as strings while ignoring case. Print `YES` if they are equal after converting letters to the same case, otherwise print `NO`. Spaces are significant and must be compared exactly. An empty line is allowed.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `Apple` and `aPPle` → comparison result: different letters become same case step by step until all characters match. Output: `YES`

tasks.title: Palindrome Word Checker
tasks.description:
A palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forward and backward. Palindromes are used in word games, toy cryptography, and simple symmetry checks.

Read one line of text with at most 100 characters, excluding the newline. Treat only alphabetic characters as part of the check, and ignore case differences. Print `YES` if the letters form a palindrome and `NO` otherwise. Non-letter characters should be ignored completely, and an empty or letterless line should count as a palindrome.

test_cases.input: A man, a plan, a canal

test_results.input: A man, a plan, a canal

tasks.title: Shift Letters by Three
tasks.description:
Cipher methods often transform text by moving letters through the alphabet. A simple shift cipher is a classic way to practice working with characters, loops, and conditional logic.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. For every alphabetic character, shift it forward by 3 positions in the alphabet, wrapping around from `Z` to `C` and from `z` to `c`. Leave all other characters unchanged. Print the transformed line followed by a newline.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `Az by!` → `A` → `D`, `z` → `c`, `b` → `e`, `y` → `b`. Output: `Dc eb!`

tasks.title: First Repeated Character
tasks.description:
Scanning a string from left to right is a common way to detect the first place where a pattern begins to repeat. This kind of check appears in input validation, text analysis, and simple debugging tools.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Find the first character that appears more than once in the line, considering the characters in the order they are read and treating spaces as ordinary characters. Print that character on its own line. If no character repeats, print `NONE`.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `abcad` → seen characters grow as `a`, `ab`, `abc`, then `a` appears again. Output: `a`
